summary: Paul addresses honor in gathered worship and corrects selfish divisions that corrupt the meaning of the Lord’s Supper.

outline.title: Imitation and Honor in Worship
outline.summary: Paul calls believers to follow Christ-shaped example and preserve fitting honor as men and women pray and prophesy.

outline.title: Divisions at the Lord’s Table
outline.summary: Social selfishness and humiliation of poorer believers contradict the shared meal’s meaning.

outline.title: Remembering and Proclaiming Christ
outline.summary: The bread and cup remember the Lord’s sacrificial death and proclaim it until he comes.

outline.title: Discern the Body Together
outline.summary: Believers must examine themselves, recognize the gathered body, and receive the meal with patient mutual care.

verses.bibleText: Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul closes one discussion and opens another by calling believers to imitation, but not imitation of personality, style, or status. He asks them to follow him only insofar as his life is shaped by Christ. That keeps imitation from becoming hero-worship. The Corinthians had already shown how easily they could attach themselves to human leaders, so this verse quietly corrects that danger again. No Christian example is final in itself. Every faithful teacher, parent, elder, or friend is useful only as a pointer beyond self to the Lord.

There is freedom and humility here. Paul is not saying, “Measure yourselves by me,” as if he were the standard. He is saying, in effect, “Walk the road I am walking, because I am learning to walk after Jesus.” The verse also reminds us that maturity in Christ is not merely about correct ideas. It is embodied. The gospel creates patterns of speech, sacrifice, restraint, courage, tenderness, and worship that can actually be seen. We learn Christ not only by hearing truth but by seeing it lived out in imperfect but sincere people.

This also prepares the way for everything that follows in chapters 11–13. Worship, gender relations, the Lord’s Supper, spiritual gifts, and love are all finally questions of likeness to Christ. The church goes wrong whenever it asks merely, “What am I allowed to do?” and forgets to ask, “What reflects him?” If Christ is the pattern, then freedom must be guided by love, authority by service, and public worship by reverence. This short verse is like a doorway into the whole section: Christian life is learned by keeping our eyes on Jesus and by gladly receiving help from those whose lives have been bent in his direction.

wordNotes.explanation: “Followers” means imitators, not personal devotees. Paul’s example has authority only insofar as it reproduces Christ’s self-giving pattern described in the preceding verses; the final model remains Christ himself.

verses.bibleText: Now I praise you, brethren, that ye remember me in all things, and keep the ordinances, as I delivered them to you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul begins with praise before correction. That matters. He is not a man who notices only failure. He recognizes that the Corinthians have remembered him and have held on, in some real sense, to what he passed on to them. Even troubled churches usually contain more grace than we first see, and wise pastoral care does not ignore that. Affirmation is not flattery here. It is truthful encouragement before necessary rebuke.

The word about “traditions” is also important. Paul is not talking about empty habits kept for their own sake. He means the apostolic pattern of life and worship received from Christ and handed on to the church. Christianity is not invented fresh by every generation. It is received. The church does not create the gospel, the Lord’s Supper, the confession of Jesus as Lord, or the shape of holy living. It treasures what has been entrusted to it.

Yet this verse sharpens what follows. If the Corinthians have remembered Paul in many things, why must he correct them so strongly in the very next verses? Because having a tradition is not the same as understanding or practicing it rightly. People can keep forms while missing their meaning. They can preserve the shell and lose the heart. Paul is therefore both thankful and concerned.

There is a gentle lesson here for the church. We should be grateful for every sign that people sincerely want to honor Christ, even when they still need instruction. And we should remain teachable, because sincere intention does not remove the need for correction. Faithfulness is not merely conserving what we have received; it is learning ever more deeply what those gifts were for. Paul’s praise invites the Corinthians to hear his coming correction not as rejection but as fatherly care.

Paul's reference to received traditions does not place human custom beside Scripture as an equal authority. Apostolic teaching was the entrusted gospel and its ordered practice. The chapter itself shows that some applications were expressed through local cultural signs, so faithful interpretation must distinguish the enduring principle from the first-century form that carried it.

wordNotes.explanation: “Ordinances” here means traditions or teachings handed on by apostolic instruction, not municipal laws. Paul praises their general remembrance before correcting particular ways their worship has failed to embody what was delivered.

verses.bibleText: But I would have you know, that the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man; and the head of Christ is God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
This verse is one of the most discussed in the chapter because Paul speaks of “head,” and the meaning of that image has been understood in more than one responsible way. Some hear it mainly in terms of authority and ordered relationship. Others hear a stronger emphasis on origin or source. Still others see both ideas touching the text in some way. What is clear is that Paul is not encouraging contempt, domination, or the belittling of women. The whole passage will not allow that, especially when it later insists on mutual dependence in the Lord.

Paul is placing men and women within a pattern that is larger than social custom. He reaches up to Christ and to God. That immediately tells us that his concern is not fashion for fashion’s sake but the fitting expression of honor within worship. Yet the comparison must be handled reverently. The relation of Christ and God is holy mystery, not a weapon for human pride. No man may use this verse to imagine that he stands toward woman as God stands toward Christ in some self-exalting way. The very Christ Paul names is the Christ who serves, loves, gives himself, and lays down his life.

So the verse calls for order without arrogance, difference without inferiority, and reverence without rivalry. Worship is distorted whenever men and women turn it into a contest over prominence, rights, or shame. Paul wants the gathered church to display relationships marked by honor. That may involve creation order, relational responsibility, and thankful recognition that our lives are not self-originated. We are received, not self-made.

The verse must be read with the mutuality of verses 11-12 and the equal value of men and women as God's image-bearers. “Head” has generated more than one responsible interpretation, but none permits domination, abuse, or treating woman as spiritually inferior. Christlike order is always shaped by the self-giving Lord who serves and lays down His life.

wordNotes.explanation: “Head” expresses an ordered relationship and may carry ideas of authority, prominence, or source; its precise nuance is debated. Paul’s three relationships cannot support inferiority, since Christ shares divine nature while willingly relating to the Father.

verses.bibleText: Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonoureth his head.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul turns from principle to practice. A man who prays or prophesies in a way that dishonors his head is acting unfittingly in worship. The exact meaning of the covering is debated, and that should make us careful rather than harsh. The basic point, however, is clear enough: even spiritual activity can be carried out in a manner that communicates disorder or dishonor. Prayer and prophecy are holy acts, but holiness does not cancel the need for fitting conduct.

It is striking that Paul assumes men pray and prophesy publicly. Worship is alive, participatory, and God-directed. Yet public ministry does not mean self-expression without reverence. The body matters. What people do with their bodies in worship can either clarify or confuse what they claim with their lips. A man may be saying true words while presenting himself in a way Paul regards as inappropriate to the setting and to the order God intends.

The language of dishonor suggests that worship is never merely private spirituality happening in a room full of others. It is relational. People’s actions affect one another. What they communicate visibly matters. That is why modern Christians should resist two opposite mistakes. One is to flatten this verse into a timeless dress code without regard for context. The other is to dismiss it as insignificant because it speaks about outward appearance. Paul refuses both. He is concerned about an outward action precisely because it expresses inward attitudes and public meaning.

Head covering carried visible religious meaning in the Greco-Roman world. Roman men commonly covered the head when officiating in pagan sacrifice, whereas Greek custom often differed. Paul's instruction distinguishes Christian prayer from surrounding cultic signals. The enduring concern is reverence and an unconfused Christian witness, not a universal command that every uncovered or covered male head has the same meaning in every culture.

wordNotes.explanation: Both actions belong to public worship: prayer addresses God, while prophecy communicates an intelligible message from God. Paul regulates how the man ministers; he does not question that Spirit-led speech occurs in the assembly.

verses.bibleText: But every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head: for that is even all one as if she were shaven.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now speaks of women who pray and prophesy, and the first thing that must be noticed is that he plainly assumes women do both. He is not silencing women here. He is addressing how women who speak before God and God’s people should do so in a fitting way. That alone should keep us from reading the passage as if Paul’s concern were to erase women from the life of worship. His concern is honor and order, not exclusion.

The uncovered head, whatever precise form that took in Corinth, was seen by Paul as communicating dishonor. He says it is like having the head shaved, which, in that world, signaled disgrace. The exact cultural associations may be difficult to recover in every detail, but the force of the comparison is plain: Paul believes that if a woman leads in worship without the appropriate sign of honor, something shameful is being communicated, even if she herself does not intend it.

Paul also guards women from being demeaned. Paul does not say women may not pray or prophesy. He addresses them as participants in worship whose ministry matters. The issue is not their spiritual inferiority but the fitting expression of that ministry. Wherever the church uses this passage to suppress women as though they were spiritually voiceless, it misses what Paul is actually correcting. He wants worship that honors God, preserves dignity, and reflects the relational wisdom of creation and redemption together.

This verse is also decisive for reading 14:34-35. Paul recognizes women praying and prophesying in the gathered life of the church; he regulates the manner rather than forbidding the ministry. The head covering was a culturally intelligible sign of modesty and family honor in Corinth. Its enduring principles remain, while the precise garment is not made a timeless condition of worship.

wordNotes.term: Every woman that prayeth or prophesieth
wordNotes.explanation: Women are plainly pictured praying and prophesying in gathered worship. Paul’s concern in this paragraph is the honorable manner of their participation, so the verse should not be made to deny the ministry it explicitly assumes.

verses.bibleText: For if the woman be not covered, let her also be shorn: but if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be covered.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul presses the point with sharp rhetoric. If a woman rejects the covering, then let the logic run all the way through: let her also cut off her hair. But because that would be disgraceful, she should instead be covered. The force of the argument is not cruelty. It is exposure. Paul wants the Corinthians to see that dismissing the sign he is speaking about is not a small neutral act. In that setting, it carried social and moral meaning.

We should hear the verse with pastoral fairness. Paul is not teaching contempt for women’s bodies or making beauty a measure of worth. He is reasoning from what the Corinthians themselves would recognize as shameful or honorable. That is, he is not inventing a random burden. He is appealing to shared perceptions and asking the women in worship leadership to act accordingly.

Yet this verse should not be used to humiliate women. Paul’s aim is not public shaming but a restoration of what is fitting. Worship should not magnify tension between men and women or invite confusion about honor. The church today may not share the exact outward symbols Corinth did, but the principle abides: followers of Christ should not make a show of independence in the very place where they are called to glorify God together. Freedom that refuses all visible forms of honor is not always spiritual maturity; sometimes it is merely self-assertion dressed up as liberty.

Paul's sharp comparison works within the honor language of his society, where an uncovered or shorn head could communicate sexual availability, disgrace, mourning, or rejection of accepted respectability. We should not reproduce the culture's shaming treatment of women. We should preserve Paul's pastoral aim: believers should not use freedom in dress or appearance to advertise moral confusion or bring needless reproach on the gospel.

wordNotes.explanation: Cropped or shaved hair carried public shame for a woman in Paul’s comparison. He uses that recognized dishonor to press the Corinthians toward an appearance that communicated sexual propriety and respect in their setting.

verses.bibleText: For a man indeed ought not to cover his head, forasmuch as he is the image and glory of God: but the woman is the glory of the man.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul explains why he thinks men and women should not present themselves identically in this setting. Man, he says, ought not to cover his head because he is the image and glory of God, while woman is the glory of man. This is delicate ground, and it must be handled with reverence. Paul cannot mean that woman is not also made in God’s image, because Scripture’s creation witness does not allow that. So whatever else he means, he is not denying women the dignity of bearing God’s image.

His language of “glory” seems to focus on how human relational life reflects honor. Man directly displays something of God’s creating intent, and woman, in her relation to man, also displays glory. Paul's point is not best heard as a ladder of worth but as a description of reflected honor in ordered relationships. Even so, because Paul’s wording can sound stark to modern ears, it must be read alongside verses 11–12, where he firmly rejects male self-sufficiency.

What Paul refuses here is an androgynous flattening of distinction. Men and women are equally precious before God, yet their embodied existence is not meaningless. Worship should not communicate that created difference has no significance. Yet the verse will be badly misread if “woman is the glory of man” is taken to mean that woman exists merely to decorate man or magnify male importance. Paul is not centering man’s ego. He is speaking of honor within a created order that belongs finally to God.

So the verse calls the church to hold together truths we often pull apart: equal dignity and meaningful distinction, full humanity and relational order, spiritual giftedness and embodied worship. When those are held together in Christ, the church is protected from both domination and confusion.

wordNotes.explanation: Genesis declares humanity, male and female, created in God’s image. Paul’s immediate argument concerns representative glory within worship and creation order; it cannot mean that woman lacks God’s image or possesses lesser human dignity.

verses.bibleText: For the man is not of the woman: but the woman of the man.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul reaches back to creation: woman came from man. He is drawing from the Genesis account to support his argument that worship should respect the order woven into human life from the beginning. His reasoning is not based on passing preference alone. He sees the created relation of man and woman as theologically meaningful.

Still, the verse should not be pressed into saying more than it says. “From” does not mean “less than.” Origin does not imply inferiority. Many profound realities in Scripture come “from” another without losing dignity. Paul is not constructing a chain in which woman is a diminished version of man. He is reminding the church that human life is received, ordered, and relational from the start.

In the flow of the passage, this verse is meant to check the impulse to treat worship as a place where all created signs and relationships may simply be cast off. Corinth seems tempted to celebrate a kind of spiritual liberty that ignores embodied life. Paul says creation still matters. Redemption does not erase what God made good. Grace does not require us to become creatures without form, sex, relation, or history.

Yet this verse cannot stand alone. If it did, some might twist it toward male boasting. Paul will not permit that. Before long he will remind men that every man since the beginning comes through woman, and that all things are from God. This guards the verse from abuse. A man cannot hear “woman from man” and puff himself up, because Paul is moving toward mutual dependence, not male triumph.

wordNotes.explanation: “Of” points back to Eve being formed from Adam’s side in Genesis 2. Paul appeals to the creation narrative for relational order, then balances the point by affirming mutual dependence and God as the source of all.

verses.bibleText: Neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul continues the creation argument by saying woman was created for man. That phrase has often been heard in a harsh or possessive way, but the creation story itself helps correct that. Woman is given as a gracious answer to man’s aloneness, not as his property. She comes as fitting counterpart, necessary companion, and strength for shared life. So “for man” here must not be reduced to servitude or male convenience.

The point in this chapter is that the relation between man and woman is purposeful. Human life is not accidental or self-defined. We are made in relation, and that relation has meaning. Paul is resisting any approach to worship that treats created interdependence as though it were irrelevant. Men and women are not isolated individuals bringing private spirituality into the room. They come as embodied persons whose very existence has been shaped for fellowship.

This verse should therefore produce gratitude, not control. If woman is created for man, then man ought to receive that gift with reverence, honor, and self-giving love, never with entitlement. The whole gospel overturns any notion of using another person for self. In Christ, being “for” another is not humiliation but the pattern of holiness. After all, the Son himself came for us, and that did not lessen his glory.

So Paul’s statement, read through the cross, invites the church away from rivalry. Men should not fear women’s ministry. Women should not consider created relation a threat to dignity. Both should see that God made human life for mutual blessing. When that is forgotten, worship becomes a place for asserting rights. When it is remembered, worship becomes a place where each receives the other as gift and honors the wisdom of the Creator.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase recalls the woman as a corresponding helper suited to the man’s need in Genesis. “For” describes God’s purpose of companionship and partnership, not permission for exploitation or a denial that both serve God.

verses.bibleText: For this cause ought the woman to have power on her head because of the angels.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s compressed statement has generated several plausible readings. The Greek says that a woman ought to have “authority” upon or over her head. It does not explicitly say “a sign of submission,” though a covering may have communicated honorable order within Corinthian worship. Others understand it as a sign of the woman’s authority to pray and prophesy. The grammar permits caution; the verse should not be made to carry more certainty than Paul supplies.

The surrounding passage establishes two firm boundaries. First, women are active participants in worship. Paul has already spoken of a woman praying or prophesying in 11:5. Whatever the head practice signifies, it accompanies her ministry rather than erasing it. Second, verses 11–12 immediately insist on the mutual dependence of woman and man “in the Lord.” No interpretation may turn verse 10 into a charter for humiliation or male self-importance.

Head dress and hair carried public meanings in the ancient Mediterranean world. Appearance could signal marital respectability, sexual availability, mourning, social status, or religious identity, though customs varied and the evidence does not allow one universal reconstruction. Paul asks the Corinthians to exercise their liberty in a way that honors God and does not make worship communicate social or sexual disorder.

“Because of the angels” is even more condensed. Angels may be viewed as witnesses of worship, servants concerned with God’s created order, or members of the heavenly assembly before whom the church worships. Paul does not explain which association he expects his readers to supply. The phrase gives corporate worship a horizon larger than the visible room; it does not justify speculation about angelic desire or female inferiority.

The abiding principle is reverent, intelligible honor as men and women exercise God-given gifts together. The exact cultural sign is debated, but the seriousness of worship, women’s acknowledged participation, and mutual dependence are not.

wordNotes.explanation: Authority, right, or freedom. The wording says that the woman has authority upon or over her head; whether Paul means a covering as a sign of ordered authority or her authority regarding her own head remains debated.

verses.bibleText: Nevertheless neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Having leaned on creation order, Paul now makes sure no one can use that argument to glorify male independence. “In the Lord,” woman is not independent of man, nor man of woman. That phrase “in the Lord” is decisive. Whatever may be said about origin or order, everything is transformed and clarified by belonging to Christ. In him, neither sex stands alone or exists to diminish the other.

This verse is one of the great balancing statements in the chapter. It prevents every proud reading that would turn earlier lines into a charter for domination. Man cannot say, “I have no need of woman.” Woman cannot say, “My life is unrelated to man.” Paul is not trapping either in self-sufficient isolation. The new life of the church is marked by mutuality.

That mutuality is not sameness in every respect, but it is real dependence, real honor, real need. The church should therefore be a place where men and women receive one another thankfully, serve alongside one another faithfully, and refuse every culture-war instinct that pits one against the other. Any interpretation of the earlier verses that cannot live peacefully with this one is not hearing Paul rightly.

There is quiet comfort here too. Human life often bends toward lonely self-definition. We fear dependence because we confuse it with weakness. Paul says that in the Lord, dependence is not failure. It is part of created and redeemed life. We need one another. That is not a curse but a mercy. Worship becomes more truthful when the church stops performing independence and starts receiving one another as fellow heirs of grace under the lordship of Christ.

wordNotes.explanation: Union with the Lord prevents creation order from becoming independent hierarchy. Man and woman are mutually necessary within Christian life; neither can claim spiritual completeness, privilege, or ministry detached from the other.

verses.bibleText: For as the woman is of the man, even so is the man also by the woman; but all things of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul explains mutual dependence beautifully. Woman came from man, but man is born through woman, and all things are from God. In one sentence he keeps creation order, ordinary human experience, and divine sovereignty together. Yes, Genesis says woman came from man. But every man since then has entered the world through a woman. Therefore no one gets to boast.

This is one of Paul’s most effective blows against pride. He does not erase the earlier argument, but he refuses to let it become one-sided. Men cannot cite woman’s origin from man while forgetting their own utter dependence on women for life itself. And both together must remember that their ultimate source is God. That final phrase settles the matter. Any human relation that ends in self-exaltation has already forgotten the Creator.

Paul also reveals something beautiful about the church’s life. Christian truth often requires both-and faithfulness. Paul neither denies difference nor permits superiority. He neither abandons creation language nor allows that language to harden into hierarchy without reciprocity. All of it is placed under the confession that every gift and every life comes from God.

Pastorally, the verse invites deep humility. Men and women belong before God as receivers. Neither brings original life to the table. Neither is ultimate. Neither may turn worship into a place of competition. When gratitude fills that space, so does peace. Men can honor women without fear. Women can walk in dignity without resentment. Both can worship with quiet hearts because their worth does not rest in winning an argument about precedence but in belonging to the God from whom all things come.

wordNotes.term: The man also by the woman
wordNotes.explanation: Every man after Adam comes through a woman, balancing Eve’s origin from man. Paul then locates both within God’s creative sovereignty: dependence runs in both directions, while all life ultimately comes from God.

verses.bibleText: Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now appeals to the Corinthians’ own judgment. “Judge for yourselves”: is it proper for a woman to pray to God unveiled? He is not abandoning apostolic authority. Rather, he is inviting them to think morally and spiritually about what is fitting. Mature Christians are not meant to obey without reflection. They are called to develop discernment shaped by the gospel.

The word “proper” matters. Paul’s concern is not only what is technically permissible but what is appropriate in worship. Some actions may be defended as lawful and still fail the test of fitness, reverence, and love. Corinth seems to have been strong on freedom and weak on beauty, order, and communal sensitivity. Paul invites them to see that worship has a moral texture. We should ask not merely whether an act can be justified but whether it befits prayer before God.

Paul expects the church to recognize something. He assumes there is a kind of moral sense, informed by creation and community, that can perceive what honors God. That is important for modern readers. Even when cultural expressions change, believers still need this habit of asking what action communicates reverence, modesty, honor, and peace in their own setting.

So the verse trains the church in wisdom. There are times when Scripture gives a direct command and times when it asks us to think on what is fitting in light of revealed truth. Here Paul does the latter. He summons the Corinthians to grow up in judgment. Worship should not be governed by impulse or by reaction against convention. It should be shaped by spiritually mature reflection on what helps the people of God pray with reverence and without needless offense.

Paul's appeal to judgment assumes that cultural meaning matters. What appeared fitting or shameful to Corinthian hearers was carried by recognizable social signs. Christians today should reason from the enduring values of modesty, reverence, marital faithfulness, and clear witness rather than treating a first-century shawl as though fabric itself possessed holiness.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul summons the Corinthians to assess what their own setting recognizes as fitting worship. “Comely” concerns propriety and honor; the appeal requires discernment about cultural meaning rather than a timeless inventory of one garment style.

verses.bibleText: Doth not even nature itself teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame unto him?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul appeals to “nature,” saying that long hair on a man is a dishonor. The exact force of “nature” here is debated. He may mean what is generally recognized in ordinary human custom, or what seems fitting based on common perception of male and female distinction. In any case, the point is not that Paul is writing a timeless handbook on hair length for every culture. He is appealing to what his hearers would perceive as a signal of blurred distinction and resulting shame.

What matters most is the moral logic. Paul thinks created difference should not be deliberately obscured in worship. There is something unwise, and in that setting dishonoring, about signaling disregard for those distinctions. Again, this is not a rejection of men whose appearance does not match a narrow stereotype. It is part of Paul’s larger concern that the gathered church should not communicate confusion about embodiment, honor, and public holiness.

The modern church should therefore resist shallow applications. It is possible to misuse this verse by turning it into an anxious policing of hairstyles. It is equally possible to miss its deeper concern by pretending that all outward signs are meaningless. Paul clearly thinks outward presentation can communicate theological disregard.

At heart, the verse calls men away from using worship to perform self-made identity. The gathered church is not the place to flaunt disregard for God’s wisdom. Christian worship should help us receive our lives as gift, not reinvent ourselves in defiance of God or in contempt of others. That was true in Corinth, and the principle remains searching now, even where the exact symbol of long hair may no longer carry the same meaning.

wordNotes.explanation: “Nature” here points to what ordinary perception and established social convention treated as fitting for male and female appearance. Since hair customs vary across cultures and Scripture knows devoted men with long hair, the argument concerns recognizable distinction and honor.

verses.bibleText: But if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her: for her hair is given her for a covering.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
In contrast, Paul says a woman’s long hair is her glory and has been given as a covering. Here again, “glory” points to honor and fitting beauty. He is not romanticizing women or reducing them to appearance. Rather, he is saying that what marks womanhood in visible form should be received as good and honorable. In the logic of the chapter, this strengthens his case that women in public prayer and prophecy should not cast off the signs associated with that honor.

The phrase about hair being given “for a covering” has led some to think the hair itself is the covering Paul means throughout the passage. Others see it as related but not identical, a natural analogy supporting the additional covering used in worship. The text leaves room for debate, so charity is wise. But the pastoral thrust remains much the same either way: Paul sees womanly embodiment not as something to downplay or renounce, but as something that may be honored before God.

This verse is tender if heard rightly. It says that femininity is not an embarrassment to spiritual life. A woman does not become closer to God by erasing her womanhood. Nor does she honor him by denying the goodness of what he has given. Her glory need not be hidden in shameful self-rejection. It is to be offered in ordered, reverent worship.

So the verse pushes back against both contempt and rivalry. The church should not shame women for being women. Nor should women feel pressure to present themselves as though created difference were a burden to throw off. God’s gifts are to be received with gratitude and expressed with wisdom.

wordNotes.explanation: A woman’s hair is given as a natural mantle and glory. The statement supports Paul’s appeal to visible propriety, though interpreters debate whether hair itself is the only covering in view or an analogy for an additional covering.

verses.bibleText: But if any man seem to be contentious, we have no such custom, neither the churches of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul ends the discussion firmly. If anyone wants to be contentious, neither he nor the churches of God recognize another practice. This is not impatience with sincere questions. It is an end to quarrelsomeness. There comes a point when argument stops being a search for truth and becomes a love of dispute. Paul will not allow worship to be continually destabilized by that spirit.

The plural reference to the churches matters. Corinth is not free to behave as if it stands alone. The local church belongs to a larger communion. That does not mean every practice everywhere must look identical in every detail, but it does mean no congregation should cherish a stubborn exceptionalism that treats the wider body with disdain. Paul appeals to shared ecclesial life as a protection against needless novelty and self-assertion.

There is also relief here. Not every contested matter in church life can be argued forever. Pastors and congregations sometimes have to say, kindly but clearly, “This is how we will walk together.” Paul’s firmness serves peace. Endless contention corrodes worship as surely as open disorder.

The verse leaves us with a final impression of the whole paragraph. Paul is not obsessed with external regulation for its own sake. He is guarding the church from contentious self-expression that disregards honor, order, and the witness of the gathered people of God. Whatever one concludes about the exact application of head coverings today, the spirit of the verse remains urgent: worship should not be driven by quarrelsome insistence on self. The churches of God are called to reverence, peace, and visible honor before the Lord.

The churches shared a commitment to orderly, modest worship, but this sentence should not be turned into a universal dress code detached from the passage's cultural setting. Paul's closing refusal of contentious novelty guards peace and common witness. It does not justify hostility toward believers whose culture expresses the same biblical principles through different respectful forms.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul closes by refusing endless quarrel over a practice meant to preserve honor and order. “No such custom” most likely rejects contentious resistance, while also locating the Corinthians within the shared worship practice of God’s churches.

verses.bibleText: Now in this that I declare unto you I praise you not, that ye come together not for the better, but for the worse.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The tone now shifts sharply. Paul can praise them in one matter, but in what follows he cannot. Their gatherings are not for the better but for the worse. That is a terrifying possibility for any church. It is possible to meet often, sing loudly, and observe sacred practices, yet leave people damaged rather than helped. Corporate worship can become spiritually harmful when pride, division, and lovelessness rule the room.

Paul’s words are deliberately severe because the Corinthians appear to assume that assembling is automatically good. Paul says it is not automatic. A church meeting is not made holy merely because believers attend it. If the manner of gathering contradicts the gospel, the gathering itself becomes an occasion of sin. That truth should keep every congregation humble. Paul also prepares us to see that the problem is not liturgical technicality alone. Something is rotten deep in the community. They are carrying social pride and class disregard into the very act meant to proclaim the Lord’s self-giving death. The contradiction is so intense that Paul says their meeting causes more harm than good.

This should move us to examine our own church life carefully. Do our gatherings strengthen the weak, reconcile the estranged, and center Christ? Or do they quietly reinforce vanity, exclusion, and spiritual pretense? A service may be orderly and still be for the worse if it lacks love. Paul warns us that God measures not only whether we gather, but what kind of people we are when we gather.

The gathering likely took place in a household large enough to host the church, and Roman dining spaces naturally separated guests by placement, timing, and status. Wealthier members could arrive earlier with ample provisions while laborers and enslaved believers came later. Paul will not allow the church to baptize those social divisions at the Lord's table.

wordNotes.explanation: Gathering as a church is not automatically beneficial. Their meal deepens inequality and exposes contempt, so a sacred occasion becomes spiritually harmful when its social conduct contradicts the gospel it announces.

verses.bibleText: For first of all, when ye come together in the church, I hear that there be divisions among you; and I partly believe it.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul has heard that when they come together as a church there are divisions among them, and he believes it in part. The phrase “as a church” is striking. They are supposed to gather as one body under Christ, yet their conduct contradicts the very identity they claim. Their problem is not mere personal tension. It is schism within the worshiping community.

These divisions are likely more than doctrinal factions here. In the context that follows, they take visible social form. Some eat early and abundantly; others arrive late and go hungry. The rich and the poor are not sharing one table as one people. So the division is spiritual, but it is expressed through class and conduct. Paul will not let the Corinthians imagine that social humiliations are separate from church life. The way believers treat one another reveals what they think the church is.

It is sobering that Paul hears of this from others. Church conflict is rarely as private as people imagine. The witness of a congregation travels. A divided church speaks loudly even when it says nothing publicly about its own wounds. That is why Paul addresses the matter so directly.

The verse reminds us that unity is not sentimental niceness. It is a gospel reality that must be embodied. Christ has made one people; therefore worship should visibly reflect that truth. When the gathered church reproduces the world’s hierarchies and exclusions, it lies about the body of Christ. Paul calls the Corinthians back to the holy seriousness of gathering not merely in one room but as one church.

wordNotes.explanation: “Divisions” describes splits within the assembled community, here made visible at the shared meal. Paul “partly” believes the report, expressing measured judgment while recognizing continuity with the factionalism addressed earlier.

verses.bibleText: For there must be also heresies among you, that they which are approved may be made manifest among you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul says there must be factions among them so that those who are genuine may be recognized. He is not praising division or suggesting that conflict is good in itself. Rather, he is acknowledging the painful reality that testing reveals character. When selfishness enters the church, some will expose themselves by embracing it, while others will be shown genuine by resisting it.

This is how God often works through sorrowful situations. He does not create sin, but he can make hidden things visible through it. A peaceful season may conceal pride or cowardice. A divisive season reveals who is willing to suffer for truth, who seeks reconciliation, who protects the weak, and who uses conflict for self-advancement. Paul is not telling the Corinthians to accept factions passively. He is saying that their presence has the tragic effect of uncovering what sort of people they really are.

There is a warning here for every church. Trials do not create holiness out of nothing, but they do expose whether holiness is present. When pressure comes, the genuine become clearer—not because they are flawless, but because they keep returning to repentance, truth, and love. So the verse should not make us cynical. It should make us sober and hopeful. Sober, because conflict can reveal much ugliness. Hopeful, because the Lord is able even through painful exposure to clarify what is real. Churches should never seek factions. But when they come, believers must respond in a way that proves the grace of God is not absent from his people.

wordNotes.explanation: “Heresies” here means factions or parties, not merely formal doctrinal errors. Such divisions are evil, yet they expose character: those proven faithful become visible by refusing the self-serving conduct that fractures Christ’s body.

verses.bibleText: When ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s verdict is shocking: when they come together, it is not the Lord’s Supper that they eat. Outwardly it may look like the meal Christ gave. Bread and cup may still be there. But the reality has been hollowed out by their behavior. A sacred ordinance can be so contradicted by the spirit in which it is practiced that Paul says it is no longer what they claim it is.

That does not mean the Lord’s Supper is empty by nature. It means the Corinthians have treated it in a way fundamentally out of tune with its meaning. The meal proclaims the self-giving death of Christ. How can a church proclaim that death while humiliating poorer members at the same table? The contradiction is unbearable. Their practice denies their liturgy.

Paul teaches us that sacraments are never magical acts detached from communal ethics. One cannot abuse brothers and sisters and then assume that a ritual performance covers the offense. The Lord cares about how his table is kept, not only whether it is observed.

This should produce holy reverence. Churches can become comfortable with frequent communion and yet fail to ask whether their life together fits what the meal announces. The Supper is a gift of grace, but it is grace that forms a people. If love for the body is absent, something essential is missing. Paul wants the Corinthians to see that the Lord’s table cannot truthfully be celebrated where the Lord’s people are despised.

wordNotes.term: Not to eat the Lord’s supper
wordNotes.explanation: The Corinthians may use the name, bread, and cup, but their divided meal no longer embodies the Lord’s Supper. A rite is contradicted when selfish practice denies the shared body and self-giving death it signifies.

verses.bibleText: For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The problem becomes concrete: in eating, each one goes ahead with his own meal. One goes hungry, another gets drunk. This is not a small liturgical misstep. It is a public betrayal of fellowship. The wealthy evidently have time, food, and comfort. Others arrive later, perhaps after work, and find themselves excluded by the selfish haste of those who should have welcomed them.

Paul’s wording places the blame squarely on self-centeredness. “Each one” goes ahead with “his own” meal. The possessive instinct has invaded what was meant to be a shared remembrance of the crucified Lord. Instead of waiting, sharing, and discerning the body, they consume privately in a communal setting. That is why the result is so grotesque: hunger beside drunkenness, need beside excess, shame beside self-indulgence.

The verse still speaks powerfully. Churches may not repeat Corinth’s exact pattern, yet the same sin appears whenever some are centered and others overlooked, whenever comfort for the powerful is protected at the expense of the vulnerable, whenever worship does not make room for the weary and poor. The Lord’s table cannot be truthful where “my own” pushes out “our life together.” Christ’s body is not a place for private indulgence.

The contrast between hunger and drunkenness shows both economic humiliation and loss of temperance. Alcohol clouds judgment and contradicts the self-control required of people whose bodies belong to God. Christian communion therefore uses the unfermented fruit of the vine as a fitting emblem of Christ's pure blood and refuses to turn His memorial into an occasion for intoxication.

wordNotes.explanation: Each person seizes private provisions instead of sharing one community meal. The result—one hungry, another intoxicated—reveals economic privilege and lack of temperance, turning remembrance of Christ into public humiliation of the poor.

verses.bibleText: What? have ye not houses to eat and to drink in? or despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not? What shall I say to you? shall I praise you in this? I praise you not.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul fires a series of questions: Do you not have houses to eat and drink in? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing? His anger is righteous because the issue is not hunger alone but contempt. If the rich want to satisfy appetite privately, they have homes for that. But they have brought private indulgence into the church and turned it into shame for the poor.

The phrase “church of God” lifts the matter far above social awkwardness. To disgrace poor believers is to despise God’s church. Paul will not separate love for God from treatment of his people. The church belongs to God, and the poor within it are not expendable extras. To humiliate them in worship is an offense against the Lord himself.

Paul also exposes how humiliation works. It is not only active insult. It can happen through neglect, through structuring community life around the convenience of the strong, through making those with little feel like intruders in a space supposedly for all. Paul sees that clearly and calls it what it is.

His final words, “Shall I commend you?” land with sorrowful force. He cannot. The church should hear that as mercy. Better a hard apostolic word now than settled hardness of heart later. The verse calls us to ask not merely whether our worship is orthodox, but whether it dignifies those who have little. A church may speak beautifully about grace while quietly humiliating the needy. Paul says such worship is intolerable.

wordNotes.explanation: Those “that have not” are believers without sufficient food or social resources. By displaying abundance while others remain hungry, wealthier members disgrace persons God has joined to the same church and table.

verses.bibleText: For I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you, That the Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now anchors correction in received tradition: he received from the Lord what he also delivered to them. The answer to Corinth’s disorder is not mere social etiquette but the Lord’s own gift. Paul takes them back to what was handed over, because reform in the church must always begin by returning to what Christ established.

To say he received it “from the Lord” emphasizes the Supper’s authority and holiness. This is not Paul’s invention, nor the Corinthians’ possession to reshape according to appetite. The meal belongs to Jesus. He gave it, defined it, and remains its center. Therefore the church does not control its meaning. It listens. There is also tenderness here. Paul is not introducing a new practice to shame them. He is reminding them of something they already know, something first given in grace. Correction is not opposed to grace; it is one of grace’s instruments. By recalling the original gift, Paul invites the Corinthians back into the truth they have betrayed.

The verse teaches the church where renewal comes from. Not from cleverness, not from cultural fashion, not from power struggles won, but from receiving again what the Lord has delivered. When church life becomes distorted, we must come back to Christ’s words, Christ’s acts, and Christ’s intention. The Lord’s Supper is not ours to manipulate. It is ours to receive with gratitude, humility, and trembling joy.

Paul receives and transmits the institution as an apostolic trust. Communion is an ordinance commanded by Christ, not a later church invention. The bread and cup are sacred emblems that direct faith to His once-for-all sacrifice; the service does not repeat that sacrifice or transform the elements into His literal flesh and blood.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul presents the Supper as authoritative tradition received from the Lord and faithfully handed to the church. The paired verbs place Corinth’s meal within a chain of testimony reaching back to Jesus on the night of his betrayal.

verses.bibleText: And when he had given thanks, he brake it, and said, Take, eat: this is my body, which is broken for you: this do in remembrance of me.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and said, “This is my body, which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.” Paul keeps the church fixed on the self-giving center of the meal. The body of Christ is “for you.” That simple phrase overturns Corinthian selfishness completely. The Lord does not hoard himself. He gives himself.

“Remembrance” here is not a bare mental recollection. It is remembering in a way that receives, proclaims, and lives from what Christ has done. The church does not merely think back to a moving scene. It gathers under the continuing significance of the crucified Lord. To remember him rightly is to let his self-gift shape the people at his table.

The broken bread therefore confronts every proud or private use of the Supper. If Christ’s body is for others, how can his people turn the meal into self-centered consumption? The sign itself rebukes them. Bread broken by the Lord calls forth a broken pride, an opened hand, a shared table, a humbled heart.

This verse is deeply pastoral. Christ gives himself for sinners, for the undeserving, for hungry people who cannot feed themselves. That should comfort wounded consciences. Yet it also summons transformed conduct. The “for you” of Jesus becomes, by grace, a pattern in the church: believers begin to live not merely for self but for one another. The Supper remembers Christ best when it forms a people shaped by his costly generosity.

wordNotes.explanation: The bread calls the church to active covenant remembrance of Christ’s body given “for you.” The emblem does not repeat his sacrifice; it brings his once-for-all self-giving death before the worshiping community so faith and conduct are renewed.

verses.bibleText: After the same manner also he took the cup, when he had supped, saying, This cup is the new testament in my blood: this do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
In the same way Jesus took the cup after supper and said, “This cup is the new covenant in my blood.” Covenant language fills the meal with depth. Jesus is not offering a mere symbol of religious feeling. He is speaking of God’s pledged relationship, secured through his own life poured out. The cup proclaims that the new covenant is established not by human promise but by Christ’s blood.

That means the Lord’s Supper is a meal of grace before it is a test of worthiness. The covenant does not rest on the Corinthians’ consistency. It rests on Jesus. His blood speaks of forgiveness, reconciliation, and God’s determined mercy toward his people. That should humble the proud and comfort the broken at the same time.

Again Jesus says, “Do this in remembrance of me.” The church must not turn the meal into remembrance of itself, its refinement, its emotional experience, or its social bonds. The Lord himself is the object and substance of remembrance. We come not to admire our devotion but to receive and proclaim his covenant faithfulness.

Paul also deepens the wrongness of Corinth’s conduct. To shame fellow believers while drinking the covenant cup is to contradict the very blood that made them one people. The cup announces a bond stronger than class, status, or convenience. Every celebration of the Supper therefore asks the church: are you living in a way that accords with the covenant you are declaring? The blood of Jesus calls not only for gratitude but for a reconciled and loving community.

The cup signifies Christ's covenant blood and is received by faith as an emblem, not as intoxicating drink or a substance that saves automatically. The unfermented fruit of the vine fittingly represents His sinless life poured out for sinners. Participation calls for gratitude, reconciliation, and renewed loyalty to the covenant Lord.

wordNotes.term: The new testament in my blood
wordNotes.explanation: “Testament” means covenant. The cup signifies the new-covenant relationship established through Christ’s sacrificial blood, joining forgiveness and belonging to a people who remember him together.

verses.bibleText: For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he come.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
As often as they eat this bread and drink the cup, they proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes. The Supper is therefore both memorial and proclamation, both backward-looking and forward-looking. It announces the death of Christ as the church’s life, and it does so in expectation of his return. The table lives between cross and coming.

This means the Supper is not a private mystical moment detached from witness. The church proclaims something when it eats and drinks. Without a sermon being preached, the meal itself says: salvation comes through the crucified Lord. That proclamation is powerful, but it depends on integrity. A congregation cannot rightly proclaim Christ’s death while living in open disregard of its implications.

The phrase “until he comes” gives the meal hope. The Lord’s table is not the final feast. It is a promise-bearing sign pointing ahead to completion. The church remembers a death, but not as those trapped in defeat. It remembers the death of the One who is coming again. That gives communion a tone of joy even in solemnity.

For Corinth, this should have been corrective. How absurd to turn a meal of crucified love and coming hope into a scene of drunkenness and humiliation. For us, the verse means every communion service should gather the church under three realities at once: Christ died, Christ is present to his people by grace, and Christ will come. That horizon should train the heart in reverence, gratitude, and patient hope.

The meal looks backward to the cross and forward to the visible return of Christ. It is therefore both memorial and Advent proclamation. Christ's resurrection is remembered within the gospel announced by the service, but no first day of the week is made holy by that remembrance. The appointed memorials are the Lord's Supper and baptism, while the seventh-day Sabbath retains its creation foundation.

wordNotes.term: Ye do shew the Lord’s death
wordNotes.explanation: Eating and drinking proclaim Christ’s death in enacted form. The Supper looks backward to the cross and forward “till he come,” holding sacrifice and Advent hope together in repeated witness.

verses.bibleText: Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Because the meal proclaims Christ’s death, to eat the bread or drink the cup in an unworthy manner is serious. Paul says such a person is guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord. The focus is on the manner, not on achieving personal worthiness before coming. If worthiness meant moral perfection, no believer could come at all. The danger lies in coming in a way that contradicts the holy meaning of the meal.

In context, the unworthy manner includes selfishness, contempt for the poor, and disregard for the church as Christ’s body. That is vital. The verse is often heard only in individualistic terms, as though Paul were mainly warning against private sins unconfessed. Personal repentance certainly matters, but Paul’s immediate concern is corporate lovelessness. To wound the body while taking the bread and cup is to sin against what they signify.

This gives the verse both gravity and mercy. Gravity, because the Lord takes his table seriously. Mercy, because the warning is meant to awaken repentance before harder judgment falls. Paul is not trying to drive believers away from Christ. He is trying to stop them from desecrating Christ’s gift through hardened conduct.

So the church should approach the table with humble seriousness, not morbid fear. We come as sinners saved by grace, yet not casually. The Lord’s Supper is not safe for pride, contempt, or spiritual play-acting. It summons us to come honestly, lovingly, and in peace with the body as far as repentance can make that possible.

wordNotes.explanation: The adverb describes an unworthy manner of eating, not a requirement that communicants first become personally worthy. Paul confronts selfish division at the meal; repentant believers come depending on Christ’s grace, not their moral perfection.

verses.bibleText: But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread, and drink of that cup.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s answer is not, “Stay away,” but, “Let a person examine himself, and so eat.” Self-examination is therefore a doorway to the table, not a barricade against it. The goal is not endless introspection that starves the soul of grace. The goal is truthful repentance that leads us to eat and drink rightly.

In the Corinthian situation, self-examination must include asking hard communal questions. Am I despising brothers or sisters? Am I protecting my comfort at the expense of others? Am I treating the church as a personal space rather than the body of Christ? Paul wants them to test themselves in the light of the meal’s meaning. Does their life at the table agree with the Lord they proclaim there?

Yet the command is deeply gracious. Christ does not say, “Fix yourself completely, then come.” He says, in effect, “Judge yourself truthfully, repent honestly, and come.” Self-examination is not meant to produce despair but humility. It breaks self-deception so that grace may be received in truth. This verse is a gentle guard against two errors. One is rushing to the table carelessly. The other is avoiding the table endlessly because one feels too broken. Paul allows neither. Believers are to examine themselves and then eat. The Lord’s Supper is for penitent sinners, not for the shameless and not for those who think repentance must be perfect before grace can be received.

Self-examination is followed by the command to eat and drink. Paul does not require sinless perfection or create a closed table for a spiritual elite. The invitation belongs to believers who come repentantly to Christ. The examination asks whether we are discerning His sacrifice, renouncing cherished sin, and seeking reconciliation with the body we meet at the table.

wordNotes.explanation: Self-examination is honest discernment leading to participation: “and so let him eat.” It is not morbid introspection designed to keep sincere believers away, but repentance that tests one’s conduct toward Christ and his body.

verses.bibleText: For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Whoever eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on himself. “Discerning the body” likely includes recognizing the significance of the church as Christ’s body in this context, not merely identifying the bread as sacred. The Corinthians are failing precisely there: they are shaming fellow believers while sharing the meal. They do not see the body truly because they do not treat the body truly.

So rebukes individualistic religion. One cannot commune with Christ while despising Christ’s people. The Lord binds himself to his church so deeply that disregard for the body becomes judgment-worthy at the table. Communion is never merely “Jesus and me.” It brings us into the reality of a people joined to him and to one another. This does not exclude reverence toward the bread and cup, but it does insist that sacramental seriousness cannot be separated from communal love. A church may have great ceremony and little discernment if it still humiliates the weak. Paul exposes that possibility mercilessly.

Spiritually, the verse calls for awakened sight. To discern the body is to recognize that these people around me—difficult, poor, awkward, wounded, beloved—are not incidental to my worship. They are part of what Christ has purchased. The Lord’s table trains the eyes to see fellow believers not as obstacles to personal devotion but as members of the same living body. To refuse that sight is to invite judgment where grace was being offered.

wordNotes.term: Damnation … not discerning the Lord’s body
wordNotes.explanation: “Damnation” here means judgment, not an automatic sentence of eternal ruin. To eat without discerning the body is to treat Christ’s emblem and gathered people as common, ignoring the communal reality the meal proclaims.

verses.bibleText: For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul says that is why many are weak and ill, and some have died. These words are startling because they show that God’s discipline can take embodied form. Paul does not speak lightly. He sees a connection between the Corinthians’ abuse of the Supper and real affliction in the community. We should tread carefully. This verse does not authorize believers to draw simplistic lines from every sickness to some specific sin. Scripture does not permit that kind of cruel certainty. But it does teach that God’s moral governance of his people is real and not merely symbolic. The Lord may discipline a church seriously when it treats holy things and holy people with contempt.

The mention of death should especially sober us. Divine patience is abundant, but it is not indifference. Corinth’s behavior is not a minor lapse in social grace. It is an assault on the meaning of Christ’s death enacted at Christ’s table. Paul wants the church to feel the weight of that.

Yet even here judgment is not presented as final vengeance for believers. The next verses make clear that God’s discipline aims at rescue from condemnation with the world. That does not make it less frightening, but it does show that the Lord disciplines his people as his own. The church should therefore hear this verse with trembling and hope: trembling, because the Lord is holy; hope, because even severe discipline may be an expression of covenant mercy meant to bring his people back to life and truth.

"Sleep" is the biblical image Paul regularly uses for death. It points to rest and unconsciousness while the person awaits Christ's life-giving call, not to a conscious immortal soul continuing separately from the body. The hope implicit in the word is resurrection at the return of Jesus.

wordNotes.explanation: “Sleep” is Paul’s biblical metaphor for death, emphasizing its unconscious and temporary character before resurrection. Some Corinthian deaths are named as divine discipline in this exceptional setting, not a rule for diagnosing every illness.

verses.bibleText: For if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If we judged ourselves truly, Paul says, we would not be judged. The point is wonderfully simple. Honest repentance now is better than painful discipline later. Self-judgment is not self-condemnation in a hopeless sense. It is agreeing with God about our sin before he must expose it more sharply.

This verse invites mature Christianity. The Corinthians are acting like people who must always be corrected from outside because they refuse to look within. Paul calls them to an inward honesty that would make harsher measures unnecessary. The church is healthiest when believers do not need to be dragged into repentance by crisis because they have learned to walk in daily self-examination.

There is freedom in that. God is not eager to strike. He invites us to come into the light willingly. Confession is mercy. To judge ourselves is to stop defending the indefensible, stop comparing ourselves favorably to others, stop hiding behind spiritual language, and simply say, “Lord, this is true of me.” That kind of honesty opens the way for healing.

So the verse teaches that repentance is protective. It is not a gloomy spiritual hobby. It is one of the ways God keeps his people near him. A church that practices regular self-judgment before the Word and Spirit is spared many sorrows. It learns to receive correction at a gentle volume because it has not hardened itself against the truth.

wordNotes.explanation: The verb means discerning and evaluating one’s own conduct. Honest self-judgment welcomes God’s correction before sin hardens; it is not self-condemnation, since confession seeks mercy and restored fellowship.

verses.bibleText: But when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
When the Lord judges believers, Paul says, they are disciplined so that they may not be condemned along with the world. This is one of the clearest statements of corrective divine discipline in the chapter. God’s severe dealings with his people are not signs that he has abandoned them. They are signs that he refuses to leave them in destructive hypocrisy.

That does not make discipline painless. It may come through exposure, hardship, weakness, or grief. But its purpose is saving, not destroying. Paul draws a line between discipline and final condemnation. The Lord’s people may be chastened, but he chastens them to keep them from perishing with the world that rejects him. There is covenant mercy even in the rod.

This verse is a comfort to repentant hearts. Some believers fear that every painful rebuke from God means rejection. Paul says no. For those who belong to Christ, divine discipline is fatherly, though sometimes terrible in its seriousness. The Lord loves his church too much to let it profane his table without response.

Yet the verse warns against treating grace as softness. The mercy that saves is also the mercy that disciplines. God does not preserve his people by looking away from their sins, but by bringing them through judgment into repentance. Therefore churches should neither despair under correction nor despise it. If the Lord wounds to heal, then even hard providences may become instruments of holy rescue.

wordNotes.explanation: The Lord’s discipline is corrective, aimed at rescue from the world’s condemnation. Paul distinguishes painful judgment within God’s fatherly work from final rejection; discipline calls the church back to faithful covenant life.

verses.bibleText: Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now gives the practical remedy: when you come together to eat, wait for one another. The cure for their abuse of the Supper is not complicated. It is love expressed in patient consideration. Wait. Make room. Refuse to turn the shared meal into a private feast. The most spiritual answer is also profoundly ordinary. This command shows that Christian worship is tested in concrete habits. Theology reaches the table by way of timing, attention, courtesy, and inclusion. Waiting for one another is not a minor social adjustment. It is a way of discerning the body. It says that no member is disposable, no poorer believer is an afterthought, no one’s presence is inconvenient to the meal’s meaning.

Waiting also embodies the gospel. Christ did not rush past us in our need. He made room for us at his cost. The church imitates him when the strong order their behavior around the good of others. Patience becomes sacramental in its own way. It makes visible the grace the Supper proclaims.

This verse is quietly beautiful for church life generally. So many communal sins begin with impatience: moving ahead without the weak, deciding without the absent, consuming before others arrive, speaking before listening. Paul tells the Corinthians to slow down into love. A church that waits for one another tells the truth about the Lord it remembers.

wordNotes.explanation: “Tarry” means wait for and welcome one another. The practical command repairs the meal’s central failure: no member should rush ahead with private abundance while another arrives hungry or excluded.

verses.bibleText: And if any man hunger, let him eat at home; that ye come not together unto condemnation. And the rest will I set in order when I come.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, so that when they come together it will not be for judgment. Paul is not despising ordinary hunger. He is separating common appetite from the sacred purpose of the assembly. If someone wants simply to satisfy bodily need, that can happen at home. The gathered church is not an occasion for self-indulgence. It is for communion, proclamation, and shared participation in Christ.

This line protects the Supper from being swallowed by mere consumption. The problem in Corinth was not that the meal included food, but that appetite ruled it. Their conduct showed they came to satisfy themselves, not to receive Christ together. Paul says: if hunger is driving you, deal with that beforehand. Do not drag private craving into the church in a way that destroys others.

His final note that he will set other matters in order when he comes shows pastoral wisdom. Not everything must be handled publicly at once. But the main issue is clear enough to state now because it strikes at the heart of worship. The verse leaves us with a searching question: why do we come to church? To consume? To be seen? To secure comfort? Or to meet the Lord with his people in humble, shared dependence? Paul wants Corinth, and every church, to answer with its behavior as well as with its creed. Worship is safe only when self is no longer enthroned at the table.

wordNotes.term: The rest will I set in order
wordNotes.explanation: Some matters required Paul’s personal presence and were not preserved as universal directions. The phrase displays pastoral judgment: Scripture supplies what the wider church needs while leaving local arrangements to faithful ordering.
